== History == The tradition began when Hase Shrine, formerly in Asami, [[:en:Beppu|Beppu City]], enshrining the deity [[:en:Toshigami|Great Age Deity]] (god of abundant harvest) and Atago Shrine, enshrining the deity [[:en:Kagu-tsuchi|Kagutsuchi]] (god of fire), were merged in worship after receiving permission on [[:en:1919|1919]] (Taishō 8) on [[:en:December 21|December 21]], and when [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] (the god of medicine) and [[:en:Sukunabikona|Sukunabikona]] (the god of water) were added, thus coming to be known as Onsen Shrine.<ref name="about" /><ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" />.
It was established using the former palace built during the crown prince era by [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]] on [[:en:1907|1907]] (Meiji 40) as the shrine building,<ref>[http://today.blogcoara.jp/natukashi/2008/02/no432-9144.html Nostalgic Beppu Tales: No432 Literary Walk] 今日新聞, February 18, 2008</ref><ref>[http://today.blogcoara.jp/natukashi/2009/02/no620-58b1.html Nostalgic Beppu Tales: No620 Postcard Feature] 今日新聞, February 3, 2009</ref> in the area of the former Beppu Park in Aoyama-chō. (This park – now the site of the Beppu Municipal Park tennis courts<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" /> and Aoyama Kindergarten<ref name="about" /> – had an area of 27.3 ha as of now,<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.visit-oita.jp/spots/detail/4518|title=Beppu Park|work=Official Tourism Information Site of Oita Prefecture, "The Prefecture of Onsen"|publisher={{ill|Tourism Oita|ja|ツーリズムおおいた|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tourism Oita|1=Tourism Oita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11318873}}|accessdate=2018-07-20}}</ref> whereas before [[:en:World War II|World War II]], Beppu Park was larger, approximately 35 ha at the time it served as the main venue for the {{ill|Beppu International Onsen Tourism Exposition|ja|別府国際温泉観光大博覧会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu International Onsen Tourism Exposition|1=Beppu International Onsen Tourism Exposition|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18339232}} in [[:en:1937|1937]].<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.beppu.biz/kongetu4.htm|title=Great Beppu Park Plan|publisher=Beppu History Materials Digital Archive|accessdate=2018-07-20}}</ref>
After [[:en:World War II|World War II]], due to the [[:en:Shinto Directive|Shinto Directive]], the Onsen Shrine located within a public park on government land could no longer be maintained,<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" />, and its sacred object ([[:en:Shintai|Shintai]]) and mikoshi ([[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]]) were enshrined at Hachiman Asami Shrine. Since then, both the opening proclamation ceremony of the {{ill|Beppu Hachiyu Onsen Festival|ja|別府八湯温泉まつり|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hachiyu Onsen Festival|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396697}} and the mikoshi procession of Onsen Shrine have been held at Hachiman Asami Shrine.<ref name="about" /> The mikoshi, originally constructed in [[:en:1919|1919]] (Taishō 8), had suffered from decades of weathering and was subsequently repaired and restored, with its completion proclamation ceremony held at Hachiman Asami Shrine on [[:en:2007|2007]] (Heisei 19) [[:en:March 24|March 24]].<ref>{{cite news |url=http://kiji.i-bunbun.com/read/read.cgi?1174748400=117478705318193=1 |title=The "Main Role" of the Hachiyu Onsen Festival Gets a "Makeover" for the First Time in 88 Years |work=アイぶんぶんひろば |newspaper=大分合同新聞 |date=2007-20-03-25 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070330180826/http://kiji.i-bunbun.com/read/read.cgi?1174748400=117478705318193=1 |archivedate=2007-03-30 }}</ref>.
Furthermore, the shrine building dismantled after the merger was relocated to Kasuga Shrine in Seikechō, [[:en:Ōita (city)|Oita City]] – the building having been lost in the {{ill|Oita Air Raid|ja|大分空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Air Raid|1=Bombing of Ōita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433002}} in July [[:en:1945|1945]] (Shōwa 20) – and was used as its main hall. After a new main hall was constructed for Kasuga Shrine in [[:en:1967|1967]], the former building has served as a storehouse for the mikoshi.<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" />
== References == === Notes === {{reflist|group="注"}} === Sources === {{reflist|2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrine|ja|温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrine|qq=}}
{{shinto-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|温泉神社 (別府市青山町)|version=98183498|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:温泉神社 (別府市青山町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:温泉神社 (別府市青山町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:温泉神社 (別府市青山町)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Beppu|WD=Q273880}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11562964]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Onsen Shrine''' (温泉神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto Shrine]] that once stood in Aoyama-chō, [[:en:Beppu|Beppu City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Oita Prefecture]]. It served as the guardian deity of one of the [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Hachiyu]]’s {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉#別府温泉|lt=Beppu Onsen|lt_ja=別府温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Onsen|1=Beppu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4890932}}, and after [[:en:World War II|World War II]], it was enshrined together at the {{ill|Hachiman Asami Shrine|ja|八幡朝見神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Asami Shrine|qq=}}, with the rites of Onsen Shrine continuing there.<ref name="about">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.asami.or.jp/about.php |title=朝見神社について |publisher=八幡朝見神社 |accessdate=2018-07-23 }}</ref><ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20">{{cite news |url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337 |title=温泉神社の意外な事実 かつての社殿は春日神社に |newspaper=大分合同新聞 |date=2018-07-20 }}</ref>.
The Onsen Shrine located in Tetsuwan, [[:en:Beppu|Beppu City]] is not directly related to the Onsen Shrine (guardian deity of Tetsuwan Onsen) {{ill|Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrine|ja|温泉神社 (別府市鉄輪)|lt=Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrine|lt_ja=温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrine|qq=}}.

It was established using the former palace built during the crown prince era by [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]] on [[:en:1907|1907]] (Meiji 40) as the shrine building,<ref>[http://today.blogcoara.jp/natukashi/2008/02/no432-9144.html Nostalgic Beppu Tales: No432 Literary Walk] 今日新聞, February 18, 2008</ref><ref>[http://today.blogcoara.jp/natukashi/2009/02/no620-58b1.html Nostalgic Beppu Tales: No620 Postcard Feature] 今日新聞, February 3, 2009</ref> in the area of the former Beppu Park in Aoyama-chō. (This park – now the site of the Beppu Municipal Park tennis courts<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" /> and Aoyama Kindergarten<ref name="about" /> – had an area of 27.3 ha as of now,<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.visit-oita.jp/spots/detail/4518|title=Beppu Park|work=Official Tourism Information Site of Oita Prefecture, "The Prefecture of Onsen"|publisher={{ill|Tourism Oita|ja|ツーリズムおおいた|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tourism Oita|1=Tourism Oita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11318873}}|accessdate=2018-07-20}}</ref> whereas before [[:en:World War II|World War II]], Beppu Park was larger, approximately 35 ha at the time it served as the main venue for the {{ill|Beppu International Onsen Tourism Exposition|ja|別府国際温泉観光大博覧会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu International Onsen Tourism Exposition|1=Beppu International Onsen Tourism Exposition|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18339232}} in [[:en:1937|1937]].<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.beppu.biz/kongetu4.htm|title=Great Beppu Park Plan|publisher=Beppu History Materials Digital Archive|accessdate=2018-07-20}}</ref>
After [[:en:World War II|World War II]], due to the [[:en:Shinto Directive|Shinto Directive]], the Onsen Shrine located within a public park on government land could no longer be maintained,<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" />, and its sacred object ([[:en:Shintai|Shintai]]) and mikoshi ([[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]]) were enshrined at Hachiman Asami Shrine. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:温泉神社 (別府市青山町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:温泉神社 (別府市青山町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:温泉神社 (別府市青山町)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Beppu|WD=Q273880}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11562964]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Onsen Shrine''' (温泉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto Shrine}} that once stood in Aoyama-chō, {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu City}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Oita Prefecture}}. It served as the guardian deity of one of the {{ill|Beppu Onsen|en|Beppu Onsen|WD=Q4890932|lt=Beppu Hachiyu}}’s {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉#別府温泉|lt=Beppu Onsen|lt_ja=別府温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Onsen|1=Beppu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4890932}}, and after {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II}}, it was enshrined together at the {{ill|Hachiman Asami Shrine|ja|八幡朝見神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Asami Shrine|qq=}}, with the rites of Onsen Shrine continuing there.<ref name="about">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.asami.or.jp/about.php |title=朝見神社について |publisher=八幡朝見神社 |accessdate=2018-07-23 }}</ref><ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20">{{cite news |url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337 |title=温泉神社の意外な事実 かつての社殿は春日神社に |newspaper=大分合同新聞 |date=2018-07-20 }}</ref>.
The Onsen Shrine located in Tetsuwan, {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu City}} is not directly related to the Onsen Shrine (guardian deity of Tetsuwan Onsen) {{ill|Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrine|ja|温泉神社 (別府市鉄輪)|lt=Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrine|lt_ja=温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrine|qq=}}.
== History == The tradition began when Hase Shrine, formerly in Asami, {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu City}}, enshrining the deity {{ill|Toshigami|en|Toshigami|WD=Q3028663|lt=Great Age Deity}} (god of abundant harvest) and Atago Shrine, enshrining the deity {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|en|Kagu-tsuchi|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi}} (god of fire), were merged in worship after receiving permission on {{ill|1919|en|1919|WD=Q2157|lt=1919}} (Taishō 8) on {{ill|December 21|en|December 21|WD=Q2603|lt=December 21}}, and when {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi}} (the god of medicine) and {{ill|Sukunabikona|en|Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunabikona}} (the god of water) were added, thus coming to be known as Onsen Shrine.<ref name="about" /><ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" />.
It was established using the former palace built during the crown prince era by {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taishō}} on {{ill|1907|en|1907|WD=Q2048|lt=1907}} (Meiji 40) as the shrine building,<ref>[http://today.blogcoara.jp/natukashi/2008/02/no432-9144.html Nostalgic Beppu Tales: No432 Literary Walk] 今日新聞, February 18, 2008</ref><ref>[http://today.blogcoara.jp/natukashi/2009/02/no620-58b1.html Nostalgic Beppu Tales: No620 Postcard Feature] 今日新聞, February 3, 2009</ref> in the area of the former Beppu Park in Aoyama-chō. (This park – now the site of the Beppu Municipal Park tennis courts<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" /> and Aoyama Kindergarten<ref name="about" /> – had an area of 27.3 ha as of now,<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.visit-oita.jp/spots/detail/4518|title=Beppu Park|work=Official Tourism Information Site of Oita Prefecture, "The Prefecture of Onsen"|publisher={{ill|Tourism Oita|ja|ツーリズムおおいた|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tourism Oita|1=Tourism Oita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11318873}}|accessdate=2018-07-20}}</ref> whereas before {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II}}, Beppu Park was larger, approximately 35 ha at the time it served as the main venue for the {{ill|Beppu International Onsen Tourism Exposition|ja|別府国際温泉観光大博覧会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu International Onsen Tourism Exposition|1=Beppu International Onsen Tourism Exposition|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18339232}} in {{ill|1937|en|1937|WD=Q18647|lt=1937}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.beppu.biz/kongetu4.htm|title=Great Beppu Park Plan|publisher=Beppu History Materials Digital Archive|accessdate=2018-07-20}}</ref>
After {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II}}, due to the {{ill|Shinto Directive|en|Shinto Directive|WD=Q3029647|lt=Shinto Directive}}, the Onsen Shrine located within a public park on government land could no longer be maintained,<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" />, and its sacred object ({{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|WD=Q327532|lt=Shintai}}) and mikoshi ({{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi}}) were enshrined at Hachiman Asami Shrine. Since then, both the opening proclamation ceremony of the {{ill|Beppu Hachiyu Onsen Festival|ja|別府八湯温泉まつり|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hachiyu Onsen Festival|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396697}} and the mikoshi procession of Onsen Shrine have been held at Hachiman Asami Shrine.<ref name="about" /> The mikoshi, originally constructed in {{ill|1919|en|1919|WD=Q2157|lt=1919}} (Taishō 8), had suffered from decades of weathering and was subsequently repaired and restored, with its completion proclamation ceremony held at Hachiman Asami Shrine on {{ill|2007|en|2007|WD=Q2024|lt=2007}} (Heisei 19) {{ill|March 24|en|March 24|WD=Q2453|lt=March 24}}.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://kiji.i-bunbun.com/read/read.cgi?1174748400=117478705318193=1 |title=The "Main Role" of the Hachiyu Onsen Festival Gets a "Makeover" for the First Time in 88 Years |work=アイぶんぶんひろば |newspaper=大分合同新聞 |date=2007-20-03-25 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070330180826/http://kiji.i-bunbun.com/read/read.cgi?1174748400=117478705318193=1 |archivedate=2007-03-30 }}</ref>.
Furthermore, the shrine building dismantled after the merger was relocated to Kasuga Shrine in Seikechō, {{ill|Ōita (city)|en|Ōita (city)|WD=Q199609|lt=Oita City}} – the building having been lost in the {{ill|Oita Air Raid|ja|大分空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Air Raid|1=Bombing of Ōita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433002}} in July {{ill|1945|en|1945|WD=Q5240|lt=1945}} (Shōwa 20) – and was used as its main hall. After a new main hall was constructed for Kasuga Shrine in {{ill|1967|en|1967|WD=Q2648|lt=1967}}, the former building has served as a storehouse for the mikoshi.<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" />

It served as the guardian deity of one of the {{ill|Beppu Onsen|en|Beppu Onsen|WD=Q4890932|lt=Beppu Hachiyu}}’s {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉#別府温泉|lt=Beppu Onsen|lt_ja=別府温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Onsen|1=Beppu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4890932}}, and after {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II}}, it was enshrined together at the {{ill|Hachiman Asami Shrine|ja|八幡朝見神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Asami Shrine|qq=}}, with the rites of Onsen Shrine continuing there.<ref name="about">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.asami.or.jp/about.php |title=朝見神社について |publisher=八幡朝見神社 |accessdate=2018-07-23 }}</ref><ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20">{{cite news |url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337 |title=温泉神社の意外な事実 かつての社殿は春日神社に |newspaper=大分合同新聞 |date=2018-07-20 }}</ref>.
The Onsen Shrine located in Tetsuwan, {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu City}} is not directly related to the Onsen Shrine (guardian deity of Tetsuwan Onsen) {{ill|Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrine|ja|温泉神社 (別府市鉄輪)|lt=Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrine|lt_ja=温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrine|qq=}}.
== History == The tradition began when Hase Shrine, formerly in Asami, {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu City}}, enshrining the deity {{ill|Toshigami|en|Toshigami|WD=Q3028663|lt=Great Age Deity}} (god of abundant harvest) and Atago Shrine, enshrining the deity {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|en|Kagu-tsuchi|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi}} (god of fire), were merged in worship after receiving permission on {{ill|1919|en|1919|WD=Q2157|lt=1919}} (Taishō 8) on {{ill|December 21|en|December 21|WD=Q2603|lt=December 21}}, and when {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi}} (the god of medicine) and {{ill|Sukunabikona|en|Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunabikona}} (the god of water) were added, thus coming to be known as Onsen Shrine.<ref name="about" /><ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" />.
It was established using the former palace built during the crown prince era by {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taishō}} on {{ill|1907|en|1907|WD=Q2048|lt=1907}} (Meiji 40) as the shrine building,<ref>[http://today.blogcoara.jp/natukashi/2008/02/no432-9144.html Nostalgic Beppu Tales: No432 Literary Walk] 今日新聞, February 18, 2008</ref><ref>[http://today.blogcoara.jp/natukashi/2009/02/no620-58b1.html Nostalgic Beppu Tales: No620 Postcard Feature] 今日新聞, February 3, 2009</ref> in the area of the former Beppu Park in Aoyama-chō. (This park – now the site of the Beppu Municipal Park tennis courts<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" /> and Aoyama Kindergarten<ref name="about" /> – had an area of 27.3 ha as of now,<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.visit-oita.jp/spots/detail/4518|title=Beppu Park|work=Official Tourism Information Site of Oita Prefecture, "The Prefecture of Onsen"|publisher={{ill|Tourism Oita|ja|ツーリズムおおいた|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tourism Oita|1=Tourism Oita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11318873}}|accessdate=2018-07-20}}</ref> whereas before {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II}}, Beppu Park was larger, approximately 35 ha at the time it served as the main venue for the {{ill|Beppu International Onsen Tourism Exposition|ja|別府国際温泉観光大博覧会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu International Onsen Tourism Exposition|1=Beppu International Onsen Tourism Exposition|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18339232}} in {{ill|1937|en|1937|WD=Q18647|lt=1937}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.beppu.biz/kongetu4.htm|title=Great Beppu Park Plan|publisher=Beppu History Materials Digital Archive|accessdate=2018-07-20}}</ref>
After {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II}}, due to the {{ill|Shinto Directive|en|Shinto Directive|WD=Q3029647|lt=Shinto Directive}}, the Onsen Shrine located within a public park on government land could no longer be maintained,<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" />, and its sacred object ({{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|WD=Q327532|lt=Shintai}}) and mikoshi ({{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi}}) were enshrined at Hachiman Asami Shrine. Since then, both the opening proclamation ceremony of the {{ill|Beppu Hachiyu Onsen Festival|ja|別府八湯温泉まつり|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hachiyu Onsen Festival|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396697}} and the mikoshi procession of Onsen Shrine have been held at Hachiman Asami Shrine.<ref name="about" /> The mikoshi, originally constructed in {{ill|1919|en|1919|WD=Q2157|lt=1919}} (Taishō 8), had suffered from decades of weathering and was subsequently repaired and restored, with its completion proclamation ceremony held at Hachiman Asami Shrine on {{ill|2007|en|2007|WD=Q2024|lt=2007}} (Heisei 19) {{ill|March 24|en|March 24|WD=Q2453|lt=March 24}}.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://kiji.i-bunbun.com/read/read.cgi?1174748400=117478705318193=1 |title=The "Main Role" of the Hachiyu Onsen Festival Gets a "Makeover" for the First Time in 88 Years |work=アイぶんぶんひろば |newspaper=大分合同新聞 |date=2007-20-03-25 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070330180826/http://kiji.i-bunbun.com/read/read.cgi?1174748400=117478705318193=1 |archivedate=2007-03-30 }}</ref>.
Furthermore, the shrine building dismantled after the merger was relocated to Kasuga Shrine in Seikechō, {{ill|Ōita (city)|en|Ōita (city)|WD=Q199609|lt=Oita City}} – the building having been lost in the {{ill|Oita Air Raid|ja|大分空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Air Raid|1=Bombing of Ōita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433002}} in July {{ill|1945|en|1945|WD=Q5240|lt=1945}} (Shōwa 20) – and was used as its main hall. After a new main hall was constructed for Kasuga Shrine in {{ill|1967|en|1967|WD=Q2648|lt=1967}}, the former building has served as a storehouse for the mikoshi.<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" />
== References == === Notes === {{reflist|group="注"}} === Sources === {{reflist|2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrine|ja|温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrine|qq=}}
{{shinto-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|温泉神社 (別府市青山町)|version=98183498|comment=Imported full ja history}}

After a new main hall was constructed for Kasuga Shrine in {{ill|1967|en|1967|WD=Q2648|lt=1967}}, the former building has served as a storehouse for the mikoshi.<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" />

{{shinto-stub}}

revision.comment: adding dummy category to make categories work
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:温泉神社 (別府市青山町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:温泉神社 (別府市青山町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:温泉神社 (別府市青山町)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Beppu|WD=Q273880}}}} '''Onsen Shrine''' (温泉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto Shrine}} that once stood in Aoyama-chō, {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu City}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Oita Prefecture}}. It served as the guardian deity of one of the {{ill|Beppu Onsen|en|Beppu Onsen|WD=Q4890932|lt=Beppu Hachiyu}}’s {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉#別府温泉|lt=Beppu Onsen|lt_ja=別府温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Onsen|1=Beppu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4890932}}, and after {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II}}, it was enshrined together at the {{ill|Hachiman Asami Shrine|ja|八幡朝見神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Asami Shrine|qq=}}, with the rites of Onsen Shrine continuing there.<ref name="about">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.asami.or.jp/about.php |title=朝見神社について |publisher=八幡朝見神社 |accessdate=2018-07-23 }}</ref><ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20">{{cite news |url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337 |title=温泉神社の意外な事実 かつての社殿は春日神社に |newspaper=大分合同新聞 |date=2018-07-20 }}</ref>.
The Onsen Shrine located in Tetsuwan, {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu City}} is not directly related to the Onsen Shrine (guardian deity of Tetsuwan Onsen) {{ill|Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrine|ja|温泉神社 (別府市鉄輪)|lt=Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrine|lt_ja=温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrine|qq=}}.
== History == The tradition began when Hase Shrine, formerly in Asami, {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu City}}, enshrining the deity {{ill|Toshigami|en|Toshigami|WD=Q3028663|lt=Great Age Deity}} (god of abundant harvest) and Atago Shrine, enshrining the deity {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|en|Kagu-tsuchi|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi}} (god of fire), were merged in worship after receiving permission on {{ill|1919|en|1919|WD=Q2157|lt=1919}} (Taishō 8) on {{ill|December 21|en|December 21|WD=Q2603|lt=December 21}}, and when {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi}} (the god of medicine) and {{ill|Sukunabikona|en|Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunabikona}} (the god of water) were added, thus coming to be known as Onsen Shrine.<ref name="about" /><ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" />.
It was established using the former palace built during the crown prince era by {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taishō}} on {{ill|1907|en|1907|WD=Q2048|lt=1907}} (Meiji 40) as the shrine building,<ref>[http://today.blogcoara.jp/natukashi/2008/02/no432-9144.html Nostalgic Beppu Tales: No432 Literary Walk] 今日新聞, February 18, 2008</ref><ref>[http://today.blogcoara.jp/natukashi/2009/02/no620-58b1.html Nostalgic Beppu Tales: No620 Postcard Feature] 今日新聞, February 3, 2009</ref> in the area of the former Beppu Park in Aoyama-chō. (This park – now the site of the Beppu Municipal Park tennis courts<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" /> and Aoyama Kindergarten<ref name="about" /> – had an area of 27.3 ha as of now,<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.visit-oita.jp/spots/detail/4518|title=Beppu Park|work=Official Tourism Information Site of Oita Prefecture, "The Prefecture of Onsen"|publisher={{ill|Tourism Oita|ja|ツーリズムおおいた|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tourism Oita|1=Tourism Oita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11318873}}|accessdate=2018-07-20}}</ref> whereas before {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II}}, Beppu Park was larger, approximately 35 ha at the time it served as the main venue for the {{ill|Beppu International Onsen Tourism Exposition|ja|別府国際温泉観光大博覧会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu International Onsen Tourism Exposition|1=Beppu International Onsen Tourism Exposition|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18339232}} in {{ill|1937|en|1937|WD=Q18647|lt=1937}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.beppu.biz/kongetu4.htm|title=Great Beppu Park Plan|publisher=Beppu History Materials Digital Archive|accessdate=2018-07-20}}</ref>
After {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II}}, due to the {{ill|Shinto Directive|en|Shinto Directive|WD=Q3029647|lt=Shinto Directive}}, the Onsen Shrine located within a public park on government land could no longer be maintained,<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" />, and its sacred object ({{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|WD=Q327532|lt=Shintai}}) and mikoshi ({{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi}}) were enshrined at Hachiman Asami Shrine. Since then, both the opening proclamation ceremony of the {{ill|Beppu Hachiyu Onsen Festival|ja|別府八湯温泉まつり|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hachiyu Onsen Festival|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396697}} and the mikoshi procession of Onsen Shrine have been held at Hachiman Asami Shrine.<ref name="about" /> The mikoshi, originally constructed in {{ill|1919|en|1919|WD=Q2157|lt=1919}} (Taishō 8), had suffered from decades of weathering and was subsequently repaired and restored, with its completion proclamation ceremony held at Hachiman Asami Shrine on {{ill|2007|en|2007|WD=Q2024|lt=2007}} (Heisei 19) {{ill|March 24|en|March 24|WD=Q2453|lt=March 24}}.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://kiji.i-bunbun.com/read/read.cgi?1174748400=117478705318193=1 |title=The "Main Role" of the Hachiyu Onsen Festival Gets a "Makeover" for the First Time in 88 Years |work=アイぶんぶんひろば |newspaper=大分合同新聞 |date=2007-20-03-25 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070330180826/http://kiji.i-bunbun.com/read/read.cgi?1174748400=117478705318193=1 |archivedate=2007-03-30 }}</ref>.
Furthermore, the shrine building dismantled after the merger was relocated to Kasuga Shrine in Seikechō, {{ill|Ōita (city)|en|Ōita (city)|WD=Q199609|lt=Oita City}} – the building having been lost in the {{ill|Oita Air Raid|ja|大分空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Air Raid|1=Bombing of Ōita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433002}} in July {{ill|1945|en|1945|WD=Q5240|lt=1945}} (Shōwa 20) – and was used as its main hall. After a new main hall was constructed for Kasuga Shrine in {{ill|1967|en|1967|WD=Q2648|lt=1967}}, the former building has served as a storehouse for the mikoshi.<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" />
== References == === Notes === {{reflist|group="注"}} === Sources === {{reflist|2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrine|ja|温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrine|qq=}}
{{shinto-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|温泉神社 (別府市青山町)|version=98183498|comment=Imported full ja history}}

(This park – now the site of the Beppu Municipal Park tennis courts<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" /> and Aoyama Kindergarten<ref name="about" /> – had an area of 27.3 ha as of now,<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.visit-oita.jp/spots/detail/4518|title=Beppu Park|work=Official Tourism Information Site of Oita Prefecture, "The Prefecture of Onsen"|publisher={{ill|Tourism Oita|ja|ツーリズムおおいた|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tourism Oita|1=Q11318873|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11318873}}|accessdate=2018-07-20}}</ref> whereas before {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II|1=World War II}}, Beppu Park was larger, approximately 35 ha at the time it served as the main venue for the {{ill|Beppu International Onsen Tourism Exposition|ja|別府国際温泉観光大博覧会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu International Onsen Tourism Exposition|1=Q18339232|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18339232}} in {{ill|1937|en|1937|WD=Q18647|lt=1937|1=1937}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.beppu.biz/kongetu4.htm|title=Great Beppu Park Plan|publisher=Beppu History Materials Digital Archive|accessdate=2018-07-20}}</ref>
After {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II|1=World War II}}, due to the {{ill|Shinto Directive|en|Shinto Directive|WD=Q3029647|lt=Shinto Directive|1=Shinto Directive}}, the Onsen Shrine located within a public park on government land could no longer be maintained,<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" />, and its sacred object ({{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|WD=Q327532|lt=Shintai|1=Shintai}}) and mikoshi ({{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi|1=Mikoshi}}) were enshrined at Hachiman Asami Shrine. Since then, both the opening proclamation ceremony of the {{ill|Beppu Hachiyu Onsen Festival|ja|別府八湯温泉まつり|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hachiyu Onsen Festival|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396697}} and the mikoshi procession of Onsen Shrine have been held at Hachiman Asami Shrine.<ref name="about" /> The mikoshi, originally constructed in {{ill|1919|en|1919|WD=Q2157|lt=1919|1=1919}} (Taishō 8), had suffered from decades of weathering and was subsequently repaired and restored, with its completion proclamation ceremony held at Hachiman Asami Shrine on {{ill|2007|en|2007|WD=Q2024|lt=2007|1=2007}} (Heisei 19) {{ill|March 24|en|March 24|WD=Q2453|lt=March 24|1=March 24}}.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://kiji.i-bunbun.com/read/read.cgi?1174748400=117478705318193=1 |title=The "Main Role" of the Hachiyu Onsen Festival Gets a "Makeover" for the First Time in 88 Years |work=アイぶんぶんひろば |newspaper=大分合同新聞 |date=2007-20-03-25 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070330180826/http://kiji.i-bunbun.com/read/read.cgi?1174748400=117478705318193=1 |archivedate=2007-03-30 }}</ref>.
Furthermore, the shrine building dismantled after the merger was relocated to Kasuga Shrine in Seikechō, {{ill|Ōita (city)|en|Ōita (city)|WD=Q199609|lt=Oita City|1=Ōita (city)}} – the building having been lost in the {{ill|Oita Air Raid|ja|大分空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Air Raid|1=Bombing of Ōita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433002}} in July {{ill|1945|en|1945|WD=Q5240|lt=1945|1=1945}} (Shōwa 20) – and was used as its main hall. After a new main hall was constructed for Kasuga Shrine in {{ill|1967|en|1967|WD=Q2648|lt=1967|1=1967}}, the former building has served as a storehouse for the mikoshi.<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" />
== References == === Notes === {{reflist|group="注"}} === Sources === {{reflist|2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrine|ja|温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrine|qq=}}
{{shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:温泉神社 (別府市青山町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:温泉神社 (別府市青山町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:温泉神社 (別府市青山町)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Beppu|WD=Q273880|1=Beppu}}}} '''Onsen Shrine''' (温泉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto Shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} that once stood in Aoyama-chō, {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu City|1=Beppu}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Oita Prefecture|1=Ōita Prefecture}}. It served as the guardian deity of one of the {{ill|Beppu Onsen|en|Beppu Onsen|WD=Q4890932|lt=Beppu Hachiyu|1=Beppu Onsen}}’s {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉#別府温泉|lt=Beppu Onsen|lt_ja=別府温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Onsen|1=Beppu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4890932}}, and after {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II|1=World War II}}, it was enshrined together at the {{ill|Hachiman Asami Shrine|ja|八幡朝見神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Asami Shrine|qq=}}, with the rites of Onsen Shrine continuing there.<ref name="about">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.asami.or.jp/about.php |title=朝見神社について |publisher=八幡朝見神社 |accessdate=2018-07-23 }}</ref><ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20">{{cite news |url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337 |title=温泉神社の意外な事実 かつての社殿は春日神社に |newspaper=大分合同新聞 |date=2018-07-20 }}</ref>.
The Onsen Shrine located in Tetsuwan, {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu City|1=Beppu}} is not directly related to the Onsen Shrine (guardian deity of Tetsuwan Onsen) {{ill|Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrine|ja|温泉神社 (別府市鉄輪)|lt=Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrine|lt_ja=温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrine|qq=}}.
== History == The tradition began when Hase Shrine, formerly in Asami, {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu City|1=Beppu}}, enshrining the deity {{ill|Toshigami|en|Toshigami|WD=Q3028663|lt=Great Age Deity|1=Toshigami}} (god of abundant harvest) and Atago Shrine, enshrining the deity {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|en|Kagu-tsuchi|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi|1=Kagu-tsuchi}} (god of fire), were merged in worship after receiving permission on {{ill|1919|en|1919|WD=Q2157|lt=1919|1=1919}} (Taishō 8) on {{ill|December 21|en|December 21|WD=Q2603|lt=December 21|1=December 21}}, and when {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}} (the god of medicine) and {{ill|Sukunabikona|en|Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunabikona|1=Sukunabikona}} (the god of water) were added, thus coming to be known as Onsen Shrine.<ref name="about" /><ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" />.
It was established using the former palace built during the crown prince era by {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taishō|1=Emperor Taishō}} on {{ill|1907|en|1907|WD=Q2048|lt=1907|1=1907}} (Meiji 40) as the shrine building,<ref>[http://today.blogcoara.jp/natukashi/2008/02/no432-9144.html Nostalgic Beppu Tales: No432 Literary Walk] 今日新聞, February 18, 2008</ref><ref>[http://today.blogcoara.jp/natukashi/2009/02/no620-58b1.html Nostalgic Beppu Tales: No620 Postcard Feature] 今日新聞, February 3, 2009</ref> in the area of the former Beppu Park in Aoyama-chō. (This park – now the site of the Beppu Municipal Park tennis courts<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" /> and Aoyama Kindergarten<ref name="about" /> – had an area of 27.3 ha as of now,<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.visit-oita.jp/spots/detail/4518|title=Beppu Park|work=Official Tourism Information Site of Oita Prefecture, "The Prefecture of Onsen"|publisher={{ill|Tourism Oita|ja|ツーリズムおおいた|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tourism Oita|1=Q11318873|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11318873}}|accessdate=2018-07-20}}</ref> whereas before {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II|1=World War II}}, Beppu Park was larger, approximately 35 ha at the time it served as the main venue for the {{ill|Beppu International Onsen Tourism Exposition|ja|別府国際温泉観光大博覧会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu International Onsen Tourism Exposition|1=Q18339232|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18339232}} in {{ill|1937|en|1937|WD=Q18647|lt=1937|1=1937}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.beppu.biz/kongetu4.htm|title=Great Beppu Park Plan|publisher=Beppu History Materials Digital Archive|accessdate=2018-07-20}}</ref>
After {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II|1=World War II}}, due to the {{ill|Shinto Directive|en|Shinto Directive|WD=Q3029647|lt=Shinto Directive|1=Shinto Directive}}, the Onsen Shrine located within a public park on government land could no longer be maintained,<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" />, and its sacred object ({{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|WD=Q327532|lt=Shintai|1=Shintai}}) and mikoshi ({{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi|1=Mikoshi}}) were enshrined at Hachiman Asami Shrine. Since then, both the opening proclamation ceremony of the {{ill|Beppu Hachiyu Onsen Festival|ja|別府八湯温泉まつり|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hachiyu Onsen Festival|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396697}} and the mikoshi procession of Onsen Shrine have been held at Hachiman Asami Shrine.<ref name="about" /> The mikoshi, originally constructed in {{ill|1919|en|1919|WD=Q2157|lt=1919|1=1919}} (Taishō 8), had suffered from decades of weathering and was subsequently repaired and restored, with its completion proclamation ceremony held at Hachiman Asami Shrine on {{ill|2007|en|2007|WD=Q2024|lt=2007|1=2007}} (Heisei 19) {{ill|March 24|en|March 24|WD=Q2453|lt=March 24|1=March 24}}.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://kiji.i-bunbun.com/read/read.cgi?1174748400=117478705318193=1 |title=The "Main Role" of the Hachiyu Onsen Festival Gets a "Makeover" for the First Time in 88 Years |work=アイぶんぶんひろば |newspaper=大分合同新聞 |date=2007-20-03-25 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070330180826/http://kiji.i-bunbun.com/read/read.cgi?1174748400=117478705318193=1 |archivedate=2007-03-30 }}</ref>.
Furthermore, the shrine building dismantled after the merger was relocated to Kasuga Shrine in Seikechō, {{ill|Ōita (city)|en|Ōita (city)|WD=Q199609|lt=Oita City|1=Ōita (city)}} – the building having been lost in the {{ill|Oita Air Raid|ja|大分空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Air Raid|1=Bombing of Ōita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433002}} in July {{ill|1945|en|1945|WD=Q5240|lt=1945|1=1945}} (Shōwa 20) – and was used as its main hall. After a new main hall was constructed for Kasuga Shrine in {{ill|1967|en|1967|WD=Q2648|lt=1967|1=1967}}, the former building has served as a storehouse for the mikoshi.<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" />
== References == === Notes === {{reflist|group="注"}} === Sources === {{reflist|2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrine|ja|温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrine|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:温泉神社 (別府市青山町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:温泉神社 (別府市青山町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:温泉神社 (別府市青山町)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Beppu|WD=Q273880|1=Beppu}}}} '''Onsen Shrine''' (温泉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto Shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} that once stood in Aoyama-chō, {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu City|1=Beppu}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Oita Prefecture|1=Ōita Prefecture}}. It served as the guardian deity of one of the {{ill|Beppu Onsen|en|Beppu Onsen|WD=Q4890932|lt=Beppu Hachiyu|1=Beppu Onsen}}’s {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉#別府温泉|lt=Beppu Onsen|lt_ja=別府温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Onsen|1=Beppu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4890932}}, and after {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II|1=World War II}}, it was enshrined together at the {{ill|Hachiman Asami Shrine|ja|八幡朝見神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Asami Shrine|qq=}}, with the rites of Onsen Shrine continuing there.<ref name="about">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.asami.or.jp/about.php |title=朝見神社について |publisher=八幡朝見神社 |accessdate=2018-07-23 }}</ref><ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20">{{cite news |url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337 |title=温泉神社の意外な事実 かつての社殿は春日神社に |newspaper=大分合同新聞 |date=2018-07-20 }}</ref>.
The Onsen Shrine located in Tetsuwan, {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu City|1=Beppu}} is not directly related to the Onsen Shrine (guardian deity of Tetsuwan Onsen) {{ill|Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrine|ja|温泉神社 (別府市鉄輪)|lt=Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrine|lt_ja=温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrine|qq=}}.
== History == The tradition began when Hase Shrine, formerly in Asami, {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu City|1=Beppu}}, enshrining the deity {{ill|Toshigami|en|Toshigami|WD=Q3028663|lt=Great Age Deity|1=Toshigami}} (god of abundant harvest) and Atago Shrine, enshrining the deity {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|en|Kagu-tsuchi|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi|1=Kagu-tsuchi}} (god of fire), were merged in worship after receiving permission on {{ill|1919|en|1919|WD=Q2157|lt=1919|1=1919}} (Taishō 8) on {{ill|December 21|en|December 21|WD=Q2603|lt=December 21|1=December 21}}, and when {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}} (the god of medicine) and {{ill|Sukunabikona|en|Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunabikona|1=Sukunabikona}} (the god of water) were added, thus coming to be known as Onsen Shrine.<ref name="about" /><ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" />.
It was established using the former palace built during the crown prince era by {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taishō|1=Emperor Taishō}} on {{ill|1907|en|1907|WD=Q2048|lt=1907|1=1907}} (Meiji 40) as the shrine building,<ref>[http://today.blogcoara.jp/natukashi/2008/02/no432-9144.html Nostalgic Beppu Tales: No432 Literary Walk] 今日新聞, February 18, 2008</ref><ref>[http://today.blogcoara.jp/natukashi/2009/02/no620-58b1.html Nostalgic Beppu Tales: No620 Postcard Feature] 今日新聞, February 3, 2009</ref> in the area of the former Beppu Park in Aoyama-chō. (This park – now the site of the Beppu Municipal Park tennis courts<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" /> and Aoyama Kindergarten<ref name="about" /> – had an area of 27.3 ha as of now,<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.visit-oita.jp/spots/detail/4518|title=Beppu Park|work=Official Tourism Information Site of Oita Prefecture, "The Prefecture of Onsen"|publisher={{ill|Tourism Oita|ja|ツーリズムおおいた|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tourism Oita|1=Q11318873|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11318873}}|accessdate=2018-07-20}}</ref> whereas before {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II|1=World War II}}, Beppu Park was larger, approximately 35 ha at the time it served as the main venue for the {{ill|Beppu International Onsen Tourism Exposition|ja|別府国際温泉観光大博覧会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu International Onsen Tourism Exposition|1=Q18339232|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18339232}} in {{ill|1937|en|1937|WD=Q18647|lt=1937|1=1937}}.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.beppu.biz/kongetu4.htm|title=Great Beppu Park Plan|publisher=Beppu History Materials Digital Archive|accessdate=2018-07-20}}</ref>
After {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II|1=World War II}}, due to the {{ill|Shinto Directive|en|Shinto Directive|WD=Q3029647|lt=Shinto Directive|1=Shinto Directive}}, the Onsen Shrine located within a public park on government land could no longer be maintained,<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" />, and its sacred object ({{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|WD=Q327532|lt=Shintai|1=Shintai}}) and mikoshi ({{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi|1=Mikoshi}}) were enshrined at Hachiman Asami Shrine. Since then, both the opening proclamation ceremony of the {{ill|Beppu Hachiyu Onsen Festival|ja|別府八湯温泉まつり|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hachiyu Onsen Festival|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396697}} and the mikoshi procession of Onsen Shrine have been held at Hachiman Asami Shrine.<ref name="about" /> The mikoshi, originally constructed in {{ill|1919|en|1919|WD=Q2157|lt=1919|1=1919}} (Taishō 8), had suffered from decades of weathering and was subsequently repaired and restored, with its completion proclamation ceremony held at Hachiman Asami Shrine on {{ill|2007|en|2007|WD=Q2024|lt=2007|1=2007}} (Heisei 19) {{ill|March 24|en|March 24|WD=Q2453|lt=March 24|1=March 24}}.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://kiji.i-bunbun.com/read/read.cgi?1174748400=117478705318193=1 |title=The "Main Role" of the Hachiyu Onsen Festival Gets a "Makeover" for the First Time in 88 Years |work=アイぶんぶんひろば |newspaper=大分合同新聞 |date=2007-20-03-25 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070330180826/http://kiji.i-bunbun.com/read/read.cgi?1174748400=117478705318193=1 |archivedate=2007-03-30 }}</ref>.
Furthermore, the shrine building dismantled after the merger was relocated to Kasuga Shrine in Seikechō, {{ill|Ōita (city)|en|Ōita (city)|WD=Q199609|lt=Oita City|1=Ōita (city)}} – the building having been lost in the {{ill|Oita Air Raid|ja|大分空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Air Raid|1=Bombing of Ōita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433002}} in July {{ill|1945|en|1945|WD=Q5240|lt=1945|1=1945}} (Shōwa 20) – and was used as its main hall. After a new main hall was constructed for Kasuga Shrine in {{ill|1967|en|1967|WD=Q2648|lt=1967|1=1967}}, the former building has served as a storehouse for the mikoshi.<ref name="oita-press_2018-07-20" />
== References == === Notes === {{reflist|group="注"}} === Sources === {{reflist|2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrines|lt=Onsen Shrine|ja|温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrine|qq=}}
{{shinto-stub}}
